coredata.dc:title: Design and preparation of a near-infrared-emissive nanosensor for the quantification and imaging of alpha-fetoprotein in vitro and in vivo
coredata.dc:creator: Hu, Yuefang
Zhang, Liangliang
Hou, Li
Lin, Tianran
Bai, Yulong
Zhao, Shulin
coredata.dc:description: Alpha fetoprotein (AFP) is an embryonic glycoprotein that is produced by hepatocytes and soft yellow sacs, which is closely related to liver cell proliferation, as well as the development of cirrhosis, chronic hepatitis and other diseases. Thus, AFP has been widely used as an important tumor marker in the basic research and clinical diagnosis of diseases such as early-stage liver cancer, endodermal sinus tumors and germ cell tumors. In this study, we developed a near-infrared (NIR) fluorescent carbon quantum dots by microwave synthesis, and constructed a turn-on fluorescent nanosensor by unique modifications. This nanosensor not only has specific response to AFP, but also good biocompatibility and high photostability. The results show that the proposed NIR nanosensor has very high sensitivity, and its detection limit is 3.0 pg/mL for AFP. Thus, it was used for the accurate quantification of AFP in human serum and the imaging of AFP in vitro and in vivo. Therefore, the diagnosis of early-stage liver cancer can be achieved by using proposed method.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Near-infrared fluorescent carbon quantum dots
Nanosensor
Alpha-fetoprotein
Quantification and imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of RCQDs and functionalized RCQDs
Characterization of RCQDs and functionalized RCQDs
Optimization of hybridization reaction time and concentration of BHQ3-aptamer
Feasibility study of the method principles
Fluorometric analysis of AFP in solution
Detection of AFP in human serum
Imaging of AFP in mouse liver cancer tissue

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos 21874030 ), the Natural Science Foundations of Guangxi Province (No. 2022GXNSFAA035469 ), Doctoral Research Initiation Fund of Hezhou University (No. HZUBS202105 ), Professor Research Initiation Fund of Hezhou University (No. HZUJS202204 ), and Guangxi Province College Students Innovation and Entrepreneurship Training Plan Project (No. 202111838032 , No. 202211838043 , No. S202211838044 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of RCQDs. The inset shows a high-resolution TEM image. (b) Particle size distribution of RCQDs. The error bar represents the standard deviation (n = 3).
X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy spectrum of RCQDs (a). High-resolution C1s (b), N1s (c), and O1s (d) spectra of RCQDs.

(a) Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy spectra of RCQDs (curve a1), BHQ3-aptamer (curve a2) and functionalized RCQDs (curve a3). (b) UV-Vis absorption and fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of RCQDs.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of RCQDs and functionalized RCQDs with or without AFP. The excitation wavelength is 419 nm. (b) Fluorescence signal of the system with different concentrations of AFP. (c) Relationship between fluorescence signal ratio (I′/I′0) of functionalized RCQD nanoprobes and AFP concentration. The error bar represents the standard deviation (n = 3). (d) The effect of AFP (300 ng/mL) and possible coexisting compounds (3 mg/mL) on I/I0 value of functionalized RCQD. The error bar represents the standard deviation (n = 3).

(a) Confocal fluorescence images of different cells incubated with the same concentration the functionalized RCQD nanoprobe (λEx = 405 nm). (b) Fluorescence intensity of three groups of cells incubated with the same concentration of the functionalized RCQD nanoprobe (λEx= 405 nm). The error bar represents the standard deviation (n = 3).

(a) In vivo imaging of AFP in a tumor. a1) Bright-field imaging after injection of 50 μL of 70 μM functionalized RCQD probe solution into normal and tumor tissues of nude mice. a2) Fluorescence imaging after injection of 50 μL of 70 μM functionalized RCQD probe solution into normal and tumor tissues of nude mice. a3) Overlay of bright-field and fluorescence images. a4) Fluorescence imaging after injection of 50 μL of 70 μM functionalized RCQD probe solution into tumor tissues of nude mice. a5) Fluorescence imaging after injection of 50 μL of 70 μM functionalized RCQD probe solution and 20 μL of 90 ng/mL AFP solution into tumor tissues of nude mice. a6) Fluorescence imaging of tumor tissues after injection of 50 μL of 70 μM functionalized RCQD probe solution and 40 μL of 90 ng/mL AFP solution into tumor tissues of nude mice. (b) Fluorescence intensity after injection of 50 μL of 70 μM functionalized RCQD probe solution into normal and tumor tissues of nude mice. (c) Fluorescence intensity after injection of 50 μL functionalized RCQD probe solution and 20 μL or 40 μL of 90 ng/mL (1.8 ng or 3.6 ng) AFP solution. The error bar represents the standard deviation (n = 3).
Schematic illustration of the synthesis of RCQDs (a) and the activation mechanism for the detection and fluorescence imaging of AFP (b).
Detection results of AFP content in serum samples from healthy human and cancer patients.

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The AFP content in serum samples were detected by proposed method.

head.ce:title: Design and preparation of a near-infrared-emissive nanosensor for the quantification and imaging of alpha-fetoprotein in vitro and in vivo

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory for the Chemistry and Molecular Engineering of Medicinal Resources, School of Chemistry and Pharmaceutical Sciences, Guangxi Normal University, Guilin 541004, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory for the Chemistry and Molecular Engineering of Medicinal Resources, School of Chemistry and Pharmaceutical Sciences, Guangxi Normal University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory for the Chemistry and Molecular Engineering of Medicinal Resources, School of Chemistry and Pharmaceutical Sciences, Guangxi Normal University, Guilin 541004, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Guangxi Key Laboratory of Calcium Carbonate Resources Comprehensive Utilization, College of Materials and Chemical engineering, Hezhou University, Hezhou 542899, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guangxi Key Laboratory of Calcium Carbonate Resources Comprehensive Utilization, College of Materials and Chemical engineering, Hezhou University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Guangxi Key Laboratory of Calcium Carbonate Resources Comprehensive Utilization, College of Materials and Chemical engineering, Hezhou University, Hezhou 542899, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Alpha fetoprotein (AFP) is an embryonic glycoprotein that is produced by hepatocytes and soft yellow sacs, which is closely related to liver cell proliferation, as well as the development of cirrhosis, chronic hepatitis and other diseases. Thus, AFP has been widely used as an important tumor marker in the basic research and clinical diagnosis of diseases such as early-stage liver cancer, endodermal sinus tumors and germ cell tumors. In this study, we developed a near-infrared (NIR) fluorescent carbon quantum dots by microwave synthesis, and constructed a turn-on fluorescent nanosensor by unique modifications. This nanosensor not only has specific response to AFP, but also good biocompatibility and high photostability. The results show that the proposed NIR nanosensor has very high sensitivity, and its detection limit is 3.0 pg/mL for AFP. Thus, it was used for the accurate quantification of AFP in human serum and the imaging of AFP in vitro and in vivo. Therefore, the diagnosis of early-stage liver cancer can be achieved by using proposed method.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are one of the most promising fluorescent nanomaterials due to their ease of preparation and purification [1,2], adjustable surface performance, superior biocompatibility, low cytotoxicity, high stability, anti-photobleaching property and emission wavelength tunability [3–6]. Thus, they have been used in biosensors, biological imaging, drug delivery, light emitting devices and electrocatalysis [7–10]. However, the existing studies have mainly focused on CQDs with fluorescence emission in the visible region, while the synthesis and application of CQDs with fluorescence emission in the near-infrared (NIR) region is rarely studied. This seriously hinders their widespread use in biomedical research and in vivo imaging [11,12].

Near-infrared fluorescent carbon quantum dots (RCQDs), whose maximum fluorescence emission wavelength is located at the NIR optical window, has weak radiation energy. The spontaneous fluorescence of organisms in this region is very low, and the absorption of light waves by hemoglobin, water and lipids in living tissues is very small. RCQDs can penetrate deep into biological tissues to generate light signals. Furthermore, they avoid background interference, and maintain higher analytical sensitivity, especially in the fields of biological sample analysis and in vivo imaging. The synthesis of RCQDs has been reported by several investigators [13,14]. However, these synthetic RCQDs still have some improved expectations, such as toxic chemical precursor use, weak NIR fluorescence emission and high cost. Therefore, it is very important to develop greener, simpler and cheaper method to prepare RCQDs with NIR fluorescence performance.

Alpha-fetoprotein (AFP) is an embryo-specific glycoprotein produced by liver cells and soft yellow sacs, which is closely related to the proliferation of liver cancer cells [15,16]. Tatarinov have detected AFP in the cells of liver cancer patients as early as 1964 [17]. Since this time, AFP has been widely used in the clinical study of a variety of diseases [18]. It has been reported that the serum AFP concentration in the healthy human body is less than 20 ng/mL [19], but higher level can be found in the serum of patients suffering from the liver cancer [20]. And in some patients with primary liver cancer AFP levels are often higher than 500 ng/mL. Thus, AFP has been widely used in the clinical diagnosis of early-stage liver cancer and germ cell tumors [16]. As cancer cells can travel through lymphatic and circulatory systems and settle in other parts of the human body, most patients require surgical removal of the primary lesion combined with chemotherapy at some point in their treatment. However, conventional diagnostic techniques such as blood sampling for tumor marker detection cannot detect micro-metastases, which often lead to recurrence after surgery. Therefore, real-time in vivo tumor imaging can greatly improve the diagnosis accuracy and increase patient survival [21,22]. Presently, in vivo fluorescence imaging is widely used for cancer diagnosis, tumor growth monitoring and image-guided surgery because it can detect the location of tumors and assess the growth of tumors [23–25].

In this study, we used loquat leaves as the precursor, and RCQDs were successfully prepared by a one-step microwave treatment of extracts from loquat leaves ( Scheme 1a). The obtained RCQDs had good water dispersion, photostability, biocompatibility and low cytotoxicity. Based on the excellent performance of RCQDs, we designed and constructed an NIR fluorescent aptamer-based nanosensor for the quantification detection and imaging of AFP in vitro and in vivo.

As shown in Scheme 1b, the aptamer of AFP was labeled with the quencher BHQ3, and the labeled aptamer was hybridized to single-stranded DNA and covalently coupled on the surface of RCQDs. As the absorption spectrum of BHQ3 overlapped with the fluorescence emission spectrum of RCQDs, in the obtained fluorescent probe structure, RCQDs as the fluorescent donor and BHQ3 as the fluorescent receptor generated a fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) effect, and the fluorescence of RCQDs was quenched. In the presence of AFP, the BHQ3-aptamer was competitively combined with AFP, which resulted in the removal of BHQ3 from the surface of RCQDs, the disappearance of the FRET effect, and the restoration of the fluorescence of RCQDs. The recovered fluorescence intensity had a positive correlation with the concentration of AFP, which could be utilized for the quantification of AFP.

Loquat leaves were collected from the Yucai Campus of Guangxi Normal University. AFP was purchased from Shanghai Link Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Bovine serum albumin (BSA), diethylenetriamine pentaacetic acid (DTPA) and chitosan (CHIT) were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich (Beijing, China). All analytical reagent-grade reagents were directly used in experiments without further purification. CEA, CA15–3, thrombin (TT), tyrosinase (TYR), dopamine (DA), vitamin B1, vitamin B2, ascorbic acid (Vc), noradrenaline (NE), glucose (Glu), folic acid (FA), triphosadenine (ATP), citric acid, BHQ3-amine and H2O2 were purchased from Boson Biotech Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). Other reagents were purchased from Dingguo Changsheng Bio-Technology Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). The normal liver cell line (HL-7702 cells), human liver cancer cell line (HepG2 cells), human cervical cancer cell line (Hela cells) and human breast cancer cell line (MCF-7 cells) were obtained from the Chinese Academy of Sciences Cell Bank (Shanghai, China). Nude mice (5–6 weeks old) were purchased from Hunan Laike Jingda Experimental Animal Co., Ltd. The DNA sequences were as follows: aptamer-BHQ3 (5′-GTGACGCTCCTA-ACGCTGACTCAGGTGCAGTTCTCGACTCGGTCTTGATGTGGGTC CTGTCCGTCCGAACCAATC-3′–BHQ3), and DNA-NH2 (5′-H2N–TTTGT-CAGCGTTAGGA GCGTCAC-3′). Milli-Q water (18.2 MΩ cm) was used in this study.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was performed using an FEI Tecnai F-20 field emission HRTEM (Hillsboro, OR, USA). A Perkin-Elmer Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrophotometer (USA) was employed for FTIR spectroscopy of RCQDs. The compositional features and crystallographic structures of RCQDs were analyzed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, ESCALAB 250Xi, USA). UV-Vis absorption spectra were collected on a Cary 60 UV-Vis spectrophotometer (Agilent, USA). Cell imaging was performed by confocal laser scanning microscopy (LSM710, Zeiss, Germany). The Bruker FX Pro small animal live imager (Bruker Molecular Imaging, Billerica, MA, USA) was used for mouse imaging analysis, and the supporting software was used for image processing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of RCQDs and functionalized RCQDs
ce:section.ce:para: In brief, 20 g of fresh loquat leaves were cut into small pieces and immersed in 50 mL of anhydrous ethanol for 4 h. The extract was centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 10 min to obtain the supernatant, which was concentrated in a rotary evaporator until slurry was achieved. The sample was transferred into a beaker and heated in a 700 W microwave oven for 5 min. The residue was resuspended in water, followed by filtration through a 0.22 µm membrane filter to obtain small particle size RCQDs. Next, 30 mL of the filtrate was placed into a 50-mL beaker, and 0.2 g of H2N-PEG-NH2 anhydrous ethanol solution was added, mixed, reflux heated to 120 °C, and stirred in a nitrogen atmosphere for 10 h. The supernatant was filtered through a 0.22 µm inorganic filter membrane. The filtrate was dialyzed (MWCO: 2 kDa) for 48 h to obtain purified RCQDs. Subsequently, 0.01 g of NHS and 0.1 g of EDC were added into 5 mL of the dialyzed solution, followed by mixing and activation for 30 min. Next, 100 μL of 100 µM single-stranded DNA, which was previously modified with amino groups, was added into 1.5 mL of the activated solution, and the RCQDs were covalently coupled with the single-stranded DNA for 30 min in a constant-temperature shaker at 37 °C. Subsequently, 100 μL of 70 μM BHQ3, which was previously modified with the AFP-aptamer, was added into the above solution for hybridization for 60 min at 37 °C to form the functionalized RCQD nanoprobe. The resulting solution was transferred into a 10-kD ultrafiltration tube and centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 15 min. The upper phase was removed, the Tris-HCl solution was added, and the RCQD nanoprobe was washed and centrifuged. These steps were repeated three times. The sample in the ultrafiltration tube represented the functionalized RCQD nanoprobe solution.

The quantum yield of RCQD was estimated by comparing with that of quinine sulfate with 54 % quantum yield in 0.1 M H2SO4, using the following equation: φx = φs(Bx/Bs)(ηx/ηs)2. The subscripts “x” and “s” refer to the test samples and quinine sulfate, respectively. The φ is the quantum yield, η is the refractive index of the solvent, and B is the gradient from the plot of integrated fluorescence intensity vs absorbance.

In brief, 100 μL of functionalized RCQD nanoprobe solution (1.0 mg/mL), 40 μL of Tris-HCl solution (20 mM, pH=7.0), and 60 μL of AFP standard solution were successively added into a 1.5 mL centrifuge tube (diluted with ultrapure water to 500 μL) at 12,000 rpm for 30 min. The solution was reacted at 37 °C for 30 min, and the fluorescence intensity was measured.

For the detection of AFP in serum samples, healthy subjects and different cancer patients human blood samples (obtained from Guilin Fifth People's Hospital) were centrifuged at 12,000 rpm for 30 min. The supernatant was diluted by 125 times using Tris-HCl solution and then stored in a refrigerator at − 4 °C. A volume of 60 μL of diluted human serum samples and functionalized RCQD nanoprobe solution were added into the solutions (in ﬁnal volume of 100 μL). After reacting at room temperature in the dark for 30 min, the ﬂuorescence spectra of ﬁnal solutions were recorded.

The HepG2, HL-7702, Hela and MCF-7 cells were removed from the incubator, and the old turbidified DMEM high glucose medium was discarded and washed again with PBS for 1–2 times, PBS was removed and fresh DMEM high glucose medium was added at 37 °C and 5 % CO2 for 24 h, until the cell density increased to about 80 %. Next, the cells were divided into three groups and incubated with 40 μM functionalized RCQD solution at 37 °C for 30 min. Prior to confocal laser scanning microscopy, the cells were washed with medium and PBS three times each. The excitation wavelength was set at 405 nm during imaging, and the fluorescence in 620–700 nm was collected for imaging.

Female nude mice aged 5–7 weeks were used in this study. All animal experiments were approved by the Ethics Committee of Guangxi Normal University. Tumor formation was performed by subcutaneously injecting 100 μL of the HepG2 cell suspension containing 5 × 105 cells. When solid tumors reached 80 mm3, different doses of the functionalized RCQD solution were directly injected into the tumors, and fluorescent images were acquired with the In Vivo FX Pro small animal imager.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of RCQDs and functionalized RCQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology of RCQDs prepared from loquat leaves was characterized by TEM, and Fig. 1a shows the TEM results. The prepared RCQDs were spherical in shape and uniform in size with good dispersion. Gatan Digital Micrograph software was used to measure and analyze the 0.32 nm lattice spacing with clear lattice fringes, which corresponded to a lattice spacing of (002) graphite diffraction, revealing that RCQDs had a high graphitization degree. After analyzing approximately 100 RCQD particles, we constructed a size distribution diagram, as shown in Fig. 1b. The size of RCQD nanoparticles was in the range of 2.5–5.0 nm, while the size of most nanoparticles was in the range of 3.5–4.0 nm.

The composition and surface bonding state of RCQDs were further characterized and verified by XPS. As shown in Fig. 2a, three typical peaks at 284.6 eV, 399.6 eV and 531.6 eV were observed in the XPS full-scan spectrum, representing the typical peaks of C1s, N1s and O1s, respectively [26,27]. The results indicated that RCQDs prepared from loquat leaves were N-self-doped CQDs composed of C, N and O elements. In XPS analysis of the element content of RCQDs, the relative element contents of C, N and O were 74.44 %, 11.80 % and 15.75 %, respectively. In addition, the high resolution XPS spectrum of C1s (Fig. 2b) showed three peaks at 284.7 eV, 286.1 eV and 288.2 eV, indicating the presence of C-C/CC, C-N/C-O and CN/CO on the surface of RCQDs. The high resolution XPS spectrum of N1s (Fig. 2c) showed two peaks at 399.3 eV and 400.3 eV, representing N-H and C-N/N-N bonds and corresponding to pyridine nitrogen and pyrrole nitrogen, respectively. The high-resolution XPS spectrum of O1s (Fig. 2d) showed two peaks at 531.2 eV and 532.5 eV, corresponding to CO and C-O bonds, respectively.

The FTIR spectra of RCQDs and functionalized RCQDs are shown in Fig. 3a. The absorption peak at 3441 cm−1 represented the tensile vibrations of O-H and N-H; the absorption peak at 1633 cm−1 represented the tensile vibrations of CO and CC; and the absorption peak at 1387 cm−1 represented the bending vibrations of C-H and N-H. Furthermore, a bending vibration corresponding to C-O was observed at 1059 cm−1. The FTIR spectra showed that there were many carboxyl groups, hydroxyl groups and amino groups on the surface of RCQDs (curve a1 in Fig. 3a). These functional groups improved the fluorescence performance of RCQDs, as well as their hydrophilicity and stability in water. Therefore, RCQDs had strong emission of red fluorescence, and good water dispersion. By contrast, the new absorption peaks of BHQ3-aptamer (curve a2 in Fig. 3a) also appeared in functionalized RCQDs (curve a3 in Fig. 3a), for example, the functionalized RCQDs showed absorption peaks with significantly increased intensities at 2893–2992 cm−1, indicating that the BHQ3-aptamer was successfully modified on the surface of RCQDs.

The fluorescence and UV-Vis spectra of RCQDs were obtained. As shown in Fig. 3b, the UV-Vis spectrum of RCQDs at room temperature had two characteristic peaks at 278 and 333 nm. The peak at 278 nm represented the π→π * transition in the aromatic ring of the SP2 domain, while the peak at 333 nm represented the n-π * transition in the CO group. The maximum emission peak of RCQDs was observed at 682 nm at an excitation wavelength of 419 nm. The red emission of RCQDs may be due to the elimination of residual chlorophyll extract by carbonization in a high temperature microwave oven (>150 °C for 5 min), during which almost all organic molecules undergo oxidation and are decomposed or modified. The intense oxidation process causes the formation of oxygen-containing functional groups of different structures and involves the adjustment of oxidation states, leading to the reduction of band gap and the emission of red fluorescence [28]. As shown in Fig. 3b, the RCQDs solution could emit NIR fluorescence at excitation wavelengths of 419 nm, 512 nm, 542 nm and 630 nm, indicating that the RCQDs prepared have NIR emission characteristics. Quinine sulfate was used as a reference to measure the quantum yield of the RCQDs prepared, and the quantum yield was found to be 19.34 %. Which is much higher than many reported CQDs using single biomass as precursor, including tea residue (2.25 % and 16.99 %) [29], soybeans (12.8 %) [30] and leek (8.8 %) [31]. This finding provide a foundation for the application of RCQDs in vivo.

This work also investigated the optical properties of functionalized RCQDs. According to the UV-Vis spectrum of functionalized RCQDs (black curve), as shown in Fig. S1, there were two absorption peaks at 300 nm and 672 nm, and the characteristic absorption peak of RCQDs was at 300 nm. The characteristic absorption peak of BHQ3 was at 672 nm, indicating that BHQ3 was successfully loaded on the surface of RCQDs. Equally important, as shown in Fig. S1, there was a large overlap between the UV-Vis absorption spectrum of BHQ3 and the emission spectrum (red curve) of RCQDs, and thus, it was concluded that BHQ3 and RCQDs are fully FRET eligible.

The stability of the RCQD solution under different conditions was studied. As shown in Fig. S2, when the RCQD solution was irradiated with UV light for 2 h, the fluorescence intensity of the solution was unchanged, revealing that RCQDs had excellent resistance to photobleaching. By contrast, as the pH value of the solution increased from 2 to 13, the concentration of NaCl in the solution ranged from 0.1 to 1.0 M and the fluorescence intensity of the RCQD solution remained largely unchanged. These results indicate that RCQDs have good stability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of hybridization reaction time and concentration of BHQ3-aptamer
ce:section.ce:para: The effects of hybridization reaction time and RCQD concentration between BHQ3-AFP-aptamer and single-stranded DNA previously modified with amino groups on the performance of the fluorescent probe were investigated under a constant temperature of 37 °C. Fig. S3 shows the trend of I/I0 and hybridization time (where I0 and I correspond to the fluorescence intensity of RCQDs before and after the addition of BHQ3-aptamer, respectively). The I/I0 value of the fluorescent probe decreased with the increase of the incubation time from 1 to 60 min. In addition, BHQ3-aptamer could be hybridized to single-stranded DNA on the surface of RCQDs at 37 °C, and the fluorescence quenching of RCQDs reached its maximum at 60 min, while the I/I0 value remained relatively constant. Therefore, the best hybridization reaction time of RCQDs with BHQ3-AFP-aptamer and single-stranded DNA was 60 min (Fig. S3a).

The effects of BHQ3-aptamer concentrations on I/I0 are shown in Fig. S3b. It was observed that the I/I0 decreased with the increase of the BHQ3-aptamer concentration, while the I/I0 did not change significantly after the BHQ3-aptamer concentration exceeded 70 μM. In other words, the fluorescence intensity of RCQDs was maximally quenched when the BHQ3-aptamer concentration exceeded 70 μM. Therefore, in subsequent experiments, an optimal concentration of 70 μM was used for the BHQ3-aptamer solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Feasibility study of the method principles
ce:section.ce:para: Single-stranded DNA shows opposite forces when interacting with CQDs. The first involves π-π forces between single-stranded DNA and the hydrophobic region of the CQD surface, while the second involves electrostatic repulsion between the carboxyl group of CQDs and the single-stranded DNA phosphodiester framework. When these interactions are predominant, the quenching group-labeled single-stranded DNA can be absorbed on the surface of CQDs, which led to the occurrence of FRET effect. If the FRET effect is observed, quenching of the fluorescence of the fluorescent donor (CQDs) can occur [32,33]. To validate the fluorescence quenching mechanism of RCQDs, we separately determined the fluorescence lifetime of RCQDs and functionalized RCQDs, and the results are shown in Fig. S4. The fluorescence decay curves of functionalized RCQDs were clearly different from those of RCQDs, and the average fluorescence lifetime of functionalized RCQDs decreased from 4.8 ns to 2.6 ns, indicating that FRET occurred between BHQ3 and RCQDs [34], resulting in the reduced fluorescence lifetime of RCQDs.

Subsequently, we confirmed the feasibility of the proposed method by obtaining the fluorescence spectra of RCQDs under different conditions, and the results are shown in Fig. 4a. Compared to RCQDs, the fluorescence of functionalized RCQDs was significantly quenched, and the fluorescence quenching rate was 76 %. Upon the addition of AFP, the fluorescence of RCQDs was significantly recovered, indicating a stronger specific binding between AFP and BHQ3-aptamer, which displaced the BHQ3-aptamer from the surface of RCQDs. As such, the FRET effect disappeared and the fluorescence of RCQDs was restored.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorometric analysis of AFP in solution
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the feasibility and sensitivity of the functionalized RCQDs as a nanoprobe for AFP detection, the fluorescence spectra of functionalized RCQDs were obtained in the presence of different concentrations of AFP under optimal conditions, and the results are shown in Fig. 4b. The fluorescence intensity of functionalized RCQDs was gradually recovered with the increase of AFP concentrations in the range of 0.006–320 ng/mL, indicating that AFP can hybridize with BHQ3-aptamer. As a result, BHQ3-aptamer was detached from the surface of RCQDs, the FRET effect disappeared, and the fluorescence of RCQDs was restored. In addition, the fluorescence intensity of functionalized RCQDs increased linearly with the increase of AFP concentrations in the range of 0.006–210 ng/mL. Fig. 4c shows the fluorescence signal ratio (I′/I′0) of the functionalized RCQDs nanoprobes as a function of AFP concentration, where I′ refers to the fluorescence intensity of functionalized RCQDs nanoprobes at the wavelength of 682 nm when different concentrations of AFP were added, and I′0 refers to the fluorescence signal intensity of functionalized RCQDs in the absence of AFP. The linear regression equation was I′/I′0 = 0.0101 C (ng/mL) + 1.042, and the linear correlation coefficient R2 was 0.9976, the LOD value was about 3 pg/mL based on 3S0/k, where S0 is the standard deviation of the blank signal and k is the slope of the linear standard curve. Compared to the reported AFP detection methods, the proposed method had advantages of wide linear range and low detection limit (Table S1). Therefore, this functionalized RCQDs nanoprobe can be used for highly sensitive detection of AFP.

To evaluate the selectivity of the functionalized RCQDs as a nanoprobe for AFP detection, the effects of tumor marker proteins (CEA, CA125, CA15–3), TT, BSA, TYR, DA, vitamin B1 (Vit B1), vitamin B2 (Vit B2), Vc, NE, Glu, FA, ATP, Na+, K+, Ca2+ and Fe3+ on AFP detection were investigated, and the results are shown in Fig. 4d. The results revealed that these compounds had little effect on AFP detection, indicating the high specificity for AFP detection based on the functionalized RCQDs nanoprobe.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of AFP in human serum
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the analytical application ability of this functionalized RCQD nanoprobe in actual AFP-containing serum samples, the proposed method was used to detect the AFP content in serum samples from healthy subjects versus different cancer patients, and the detection results are shown in Table 1. The content of AFP in serum samples of healthy subjects was similar with that of.

cervical carcinoma and breast cancer patients, while the content of AFP in serum samples of liver cancer patients was ten times that of healthy subjects. It has been reported that the serum AFP concentration in the healthy human body is less than 20 ng/mL [19], indicating that the functionalized RCQD nanoprobe can be used in the analysis of actual AFP-containing serum samples. The standard recovery rate for AFP detection was between 95.0 % and 109 %, which met the clinical analysis requirements, indicating that the proposed method has broad application prospects for the determination of AFP concentration. It can be used in the early diagnosis of liver cancer patients and the specific identification with other cancers.

To explore the biocompatibility and biological application of RCQDs, a cytotoxicity test was conducted in this study, and the cytotoxicity of RCQDs was determined using the HepG2 cell line. As shown in Fig. S5, when the concentration of RCQDs was less than 300 μg/mL, the cell survival rate was greater than 90 %, indicating that RCQDs have low cytotoxicity and good biocompatibility, and great application potential in the field of live cell imaging. To further investigate the potential of the functionalized RCQD nanoprobe for AFP detection in live cells, we performed cells imaging studies of HepG2, HL-7702, Hela and MCF-7 cells by laser confocal microscopy, the results were shown in Fig. 5a. When HepG2 cells were incubated with the functionalized RCQD nanoprobe, red fluorescence was observed in HepG2 cells with the excitation at 405 nm. When HL-7702, Hela and MCF-7 cells were incubated with the same concentration of the functionalized RCQD nanoprobe, very weak red fluorescence could be observed in all three type cells. Fig. 5b shows the fluorescence intensity of various cells with excitation at 405 nm. These results indicated that the fluorescence intensity of HepG2 cells was the strongest, which was approximately two times stronger than that of HL-7702, Hela and MCF-7 cells. The reason why the fluorescence intensity of HepG2 cell group was stronger than that of the other cell types was that the AFP content in HepG2 cells is high. The cell imaging results indicate that the functionalized RCQD nanoprobe prepared can be used for the specific recognition of hepatoma carcinoma cell.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Imaging of AFP in mouse liver cancer tissue
ce:section.ce:para: To further investigate the feasibility and application potential of the functionalized RCQD nanoprobe for the specific detection of AFP, the AFP content in mouse liver cancer tissues was investigated by in vivo fluorescence imaging. The functional RCQD nanoprobe was directly injected into the tumor tissue. For the control, non-tumor site were also injected with the same concentration of the functional RCQD nanoprobe, and after the mice were anesthetized, in vivo fluorescence imaging of non-tumor site and the tumor tissue were performed. The results are shown in Fig. 6a1–3. The fluorescence intensity of the tumor tissue site was stronger than that of the normal tissue site due to the reaction between the functionalized RCQD nanoprobe and the AFP in tumor tissue ( Fig. 6b). To confirm that the increased fluorescence of the tumor tissue was due to the specific binding of the functionalized RCQD nanoprobe to AFP, the functionalized RCQD nanoprobe (50 μL of 70 μM solution) was injected into the tumor tissue. Subsequently, different concentrations of AFP solution were injected into the tumor tissue, and fluorescence imaging was performed. Its fluorescence intensity was compared (Fig. 6a4–6). It was observed that the fluorescence at the tumor site was enhanced after the injection of different concentrations of AFP, and the fluorescence intensity increased gradually with the increase of AFP concentrations (Fig. 6c). The increased fluorescence at the tumor site was caused by the specific binding of the aptamer and AFP in the tumor tissue. Which indicate that the functionalized RCQD can be used as a fluorescent nanoprobe for specific imaging of AFP in mouse liver cancer tissue.

In summary, NIR-emissive CQDs were prepared by a one-step microwave green synthesis method. Using the disease marker AFP for liver cancer as a target, we developed a fluorescent nanosensor to specifically detect AFP based on the principle of "off-on". This probe has the advantages of good selectivity, wide linear range and low detection limit, and it was applied for the determination of the AFP concentration in the serum of different cancer patients and healthy subjects. In particular, based on the NIR emission characteristics of the nanosensor, the AFP-based specific fluorescence imaging for liver cancer tissue was realized, indicating that the probe has a good application potential in the fields of biomedical research and clinical diagnosis. This study also provides a new paradigm for applying natural biomass to these fields. It may also open up new avenues for the early diagnosis of liver cancer, as well as monitoring treatment efficacy and prognosis.

Yuefang Hu: Methodology, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Liangliang Zhang: Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Li Hou: Conceived and designed the study. Tianran Lin: Methodology, Conceptualization. Yulong Bai: Investigation, Editing, Supervision. Shulin Zhao: Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos 21874030), the Natural Science Foundations of Guangxi Province (No.2022GXNSFAA035469), Doctoral Research Initiation Fund of Hezhou University (No. HZUBS202105), Professor Research Initiation Fund of Hezhou University (No. HZUJS202204), and Guangxi Province College Students Innovation and Entrepreneurship Training Plan Project (No.202111838032, No.202211838043, No.S202211838044).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2022.133256.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Yuefang Hu is a professor in Hezhou University. She received his Ph.D. degree in Analytical Chemistry from Guangxi Normal University, China. His research interests are synthesis of nanomaterials, fluorescent sensor and biological imaging.

Liangliang Zhang is a professor. He received both his M.S. and Ph.D. degrees in Analytical Chemistry from Hunan University in 2009 and 2012, respectively. Now, he works in Guangxi Normal University and majors in chemical and biological sensing technology.

Li Hou joined Guangxi Normal University (China) as a teacher since 2017 and received a Ph.D. degree in Pharmaceutical Analysis from Fuzhou University in 2017. Currently, she serves as a lecturer in Analytical Chemistry at Guangxi Normal University. Her research interests mainly focus on optical and electrochemical biosensors and their application in food safety, clinical diagnosis, and environmental analysis.

Tianran Lin obtained a Ph.D. degree in Analytical Chemistry from Fuzhou University in 2017. Now He serves as a lecturer in Analytical Chemistry at Guangxi Normal University. His research interest is the synthesis and application of functional nanomaterials for analysis.
Yulong Bai received his M.S. degree in Analytical Chemistry from Guangxi Normal University, China. His research interests are synthesis of nanomaterials, fluorescent sensor and biological imaging.

Shulin Zhao is a professor. He received his B.S. degree in Analytical Chemistry from Hunan University, and Ph. D. degree in Chemical Engineering from Sichuan University. Now, he works in Guangxi Normal University and majors in synthesis of nanomaterials, fluorescent sensor and biological imaging. He has published over 400 research papers.
